title: Chapter 15: The Return Question
content: <!-- Speed: Breather | D3+D1 -->
"You got a job offer," Chloe said. It wasn't a question.

Sam had been staring at her inbox for fifteen minutes. The email had arrived at 8 AM from a CFO at a mid-size fintech company in San Francisco. Subject line: *Your Spreadsheet Went Viral and We Want to Hire You.*

"They saw the TikTok," Sam said. "Someone posted a screenshot of my spreadsheet. The one with 'Souls Liberated' and 'Probable Arrest Warrants.' They said it's 'the most elegant financial tracking they've seen applied to a non-financial context.'"
"They want to hire you because of a spreadsheet about exorcising influencers."

"They want to hire me because they think I can 'bring unconventional analytical rigor to traditional financial operations.' Which is corporate-speak for 'your spreadsheet made us laugh and we're desperate for people who aren't dead inside.'"
Chloe nodded. "Will you take it?"
Sam closed her laptop. She looked at the ocean. The ocean, as always, was aggressively neutral on the subject of career decisions.
"I don't know," Sam said. "A month ago, I would have said yes immediately. 401(k), salary, title. Everything I was supposed to want."
"And now?"
"Now I have a column in my spreadsheet called 'Things That Actually Matter' and it has one entry and that entry is not a 401(k)."
"What is it?"
"You know what it is."
Chloe did know. She looked at the ocean too. For a moment they were just two women staring at water, which is one of the oldest human activities and one of the best.
"I got an offer too," Chloe said.
Sam turned. "What kind?"
"A speaking invitation. Some kind of conference for women in tech. They want me to give a keynote. 'Finding Your Main Character Energy in a World That Wants You to Be an NPC.'"
"That's a terrible title."
"I didn't write it. They did. They're paying fifteen thousand dollars."
Sam's eyebrows went up. "Are you going to do it?"
"I don't know. It's money. It's a platform. It's also... a stage. And stages are how you become a performer instead of a person."
"You've been performing this whole time."
"No. I've been living. I've just been living in a way that other people find entertaining. There's a difference."
Sam thought about this. "The difference is whether you'd still do it if no one was watching."
"Exactly."

They sat in silence for a long time. The carousel played its loop. The seagulls continued their eternal war against human snacks. The Pacific arrived and retreated, arrived and retreated, the most reliable performance on earth.
"What if we did both?" Sam said finally.
"Both?"
"What if I take the job — but I don't leave the kingdom. What if you give the keynote — but you don't become a brand. What if we just... take the kingdom with us?"
Chloe considered this. The crown was tilted at its usual 45-degree angle. The sword was leaning against the bench. The ocean was being the ocean.

"Avalon was never a place," Chloe said slowly. "It was a way of looking. San Francisco was Avalon — I just couldn't see it through all the Zoom meetings. The gas station was Avalon. The motel was Avalon. The beach is Avalon."
"So?"
"So if Avalon is a way of looking, you can take a way of looking anywhere. You can take it to a fintech office. You can take it to a conference keynote. You can take it back to San Francisco."
Sam nodded. "The kingdom is portable."
"The kingdom has always been portable. The crown is just plastic. The sword is just foam. The only thing that's real is the way you see things after you've seen them differently."
Sam added a new column to the spreadsheet. Column O: "Portable Kingdom — Feasibility Assessment." Value: "Confirmed."
"So we're going back?" Sam asked.
"We're going forward. It just happens to be north."
Sam closed her laptop. "I'll reply to the fintech company. And tell them I have one condition."
"What condition?"
"I work remotely. Two days a week. The other three, I'm at the pier. Someone has to run the spreadsheet."
Chloe smiled. "That's my Royal Treasurer."
